b5.title: Block 5
b5.sub: A/B test, Brand lift & Conversion Lift

cards.body: Day 6 enters creative and measurement, but everything builds on Days 1-5. Retrieve the mental anchors before moving on.

rows: Awareness · Traffic · Engagement · Leads · App · Sales
Complementary. Without both you lose 10-30% to ATT and ad blockers.
Custom = retargeting (they already know you). LAL = prospecting (similar).

cards.q: What is the correct objective to sell on a website?
options: Sales with the Purchase conversion event
cards.explanation: For a transaction on a site: Sales with Purchase. Traffic optimizes for clicks (not purchases), Awareness for recall.

cards.q: Custom Audience vs Lookalike — the key difference:
options: They are the same, different name
Custom is offline only; LAL online only
cards.explanation: Custom Audience = people who ALREADY interacted (retargeting). Lookalike = NEW people similar to a source (prospecting).

cards.q: Changing the bid strategy in the middle of the learning phase:
options: Has no effect on the campaign
Resets learning and you start from zero
Only affects the ad set budget
cards.explanation: Significant changes reset learning. You have to wait another 50 events/7 days.

cards.q: The modern winning audience combination:
cards.explanation: Best practice: let the algorithm find the audience. Broad + Advantage+ Audience ON gives maximum room; lookalike and interests pass as suggestions.

cards.q: Pixel and CAPI are:
options: The same, only the name changes
Substitutes: use one or the other
Complementary: it's recommended to use both
cards.explanation: Pixel (browser) + CAPI (server) cover ~95%. Pixel only loses 10-30% to ATT and ad blockers.

cards.body: Ready. Day 6 shows you how to communicate to that audience (creative: formats, placements, mobile-first) and how to measure whether it worked (KPIs, attribution, A/B test, Brand/Conversion Lift).

lessons.title: Explainer · Ad formats and Instant Experience

cards.body: Before getting into creative and measurement, recall the audiences material from memory. Forcing recall before rereading (effortful retrieval) is what fixes knowledge for the long term. If you miss any, don't worry: mistakes teach too.

The ad format is the visual mold with which your ad is built: it defines how it looks and how many pieces of media (visual content, that is images or videos) it holds inside. Don't confuse the format with the message or the audience: the format is just the container, the box where you'll put your creativity. The reason it matters so much is that each campaign objective calls for a different container — presenting a single product is not the same as telling a story with five. That's why the 410 exam often asks which format you'd choose based on what you want to achieve. Master it here and you won't miss those anymore.

rows: Meta automatically chooses which media to deliver (single/carousel/etc.) based on performance
A single asset. The simplest and most common format.
2+ images or videos in a horizontal swipe. Ideal for multiple products or storytelling.
Hero video or image + grid of catalog products below. Mobile only. Ideal with a catalog.

cards.body:
Memorize each container with its typical use:
1. Flexible: you upload several pieces and Meta automatically decides which to deliver (an image, a carousel, etc.) based on which is performing best.
2. Single image or video: a single piece, the simplest and most common format.
3. Carousel: 2 to 10 cards the user swipes sideways with their finger; ideal for multiple products or to tell a story step by step.
4. Collection: a hero (the large main piece at the top, video or image) followed by a grid of catalog products below; mobile only.

The Instant Experience (formerly called Canvas) is a mini-page that opens full-screen inside Meta as soon as the user taps your ad. The word "instant" comes from the fact that it loads instantly, without the wait of an external website. It only exists on mobile: it doesn't appear on desktop. It's a post-click experience (what happens after the person clicks): you can include videos, photos, buttons and a catalog, and the user never leaves the Facebook or Instagram app. That reduces friction and prevents you from losing people on a slow site.

There are three classic situations where it's worth it. 1. Your website loads slowly or looks bad on mobile (it's not mobile-friendly, that is, not adapted to small screens): the Instant Experience saves you because it lives inside Meta. 2. You want to tell your whole story — photos, video, text — without taking the user out of the app, where it's easier for them to get distracted and leave. 3. You have a catalog (the list of products you upload to Meta) and you want to show the full assortment without sending anyone to an external site. Example: Pinwheel Toy Store has a site that's slow to load; it builds an Instant Experience with its holiday video and the toy catalog below, so nobody abandons due to slowness.

cards.label: PRO TIP
cards.body: Flexible is a good option when you do NOT know which format performs best. Meta tests several and delivers the winner. It's like Advantage+ but at the format level.

Urban Store (youth clothing) has to choose a container based on what it wants to show. If it only has a nice editorial video of the season, it uses Single video. If it wants to show off 5 outfits the customer swipes through, it uses Carousel. If it wants to open with a big video telling the story of the collection and below show the assortment of SKUs (each individual catalog product, from the English Stock Keeping Unit), it uses Collection. And if its site loads slowly on mobile, it puts everything in an Instant Experience so the buyer never leaves Meta.

cards.h: How this looks in Ads Manager (step by step)
cards.body: The format is chosen last, at the ad level (the third tier, after campaign and ad set). The path is: Ads Manager → open your ad → Ad setup section → Format. There Meta shows you the buttons Single image or video, Carousel and Collection. If you turn on Flexible, instead of choosing one you upload several pieces and let the system rotate and deliver the best. The Instant Experience appears further down as an "Add an Instant Experience" checkbox that enriches the post-click experience. Example: Urban Store goes to Format, picks Collection, connects its catalog and chooses the video that will be the hero.

cards.q: Which format shows a hero (video/image) on top + a grid of catalog products below?
cards.explanation: Collection = hero + product grid. Ideal with a catalog. Carousel is a horizontal swipe without a grid.

cards.q: The format where Meta automatically chooses which media to deliver based on performance is ___.
cards.explanation: Flexible lets Meta test and deliver the winning format. Useful when you don't know which performs best.

cards.q: You want to show 5 outfits from the collection at the same time in a swipe format. Which do you use?
cards.explanation: Carousel supports 2-10 cards in a swipe. Each card can be different. Ideal for multiple products.

cards.q: Instant Experience only works on ___ devices and placements.
cards.explanation: Instant Experience is full-screen post-click mobile only. It doesn't exist on desktop.

cards.q: In which case is it best to use Instant Experience?
options: When the site is slow or you want everything inside Meta
When you want to drive traffic to an external landing page
When the campaign objective is pure Awareness
Only when the placement is desktop
cards.explanation: Instant Experience is full-screen post-click mobile only. Useful if your website isn't mobile-friendly or you want to keep the user inside Meta.

cards.q: The simplest format, with a single asset, is ___ image or video.
cards.explanation: Single image/video = a single asset. The simplest and most common format.

cards.q: Collection is only available on:
cards.explanation: Collection (hero + grid) is mobile only. Ideal with a catalog to show products without taking the user out.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.body: Urban Store is launching its new winter collection. It has an editorial video with the model walking through a loft and wants to tell that story and also show the grid of SKUs (coat, sweater, boots) from the catalog, all inside Meta and without sending the user to a slow external site.

cards.q: Why Collection and not Carousel in this case?
options: Collection gives a big hero + grid; Carousel doesn't
Because Carousel doesn't support a connected catalog
Because Collection and Carousel are the same
cards.explanation: Collection = hero + grid (storytelling + catalog in one). Carousel = swipe only without a hero. For this case (tell a story + show SKUs), Collection is optimal.

cards.q: Urban Store's site loads slowly on mobile. Which tool avoids that friction while keeping the user inside Meta?
options: Drive traffic to the website anyway
Switch to the desktop placement only
Use a Single image without any link
cards.explanation: Instant Experience loads instantly inside Meta. It solves exactly the problem of a slow website on mobile.

cards.q: Urban Store doesn't know whether Collection or Carousel will perform better with this audience. Which option lets Meta decide?
options: Flexible: Meta delivers the winning format
cards.explanation: Flexible lets Meta deliver the best-performing format per person. It's like Advantage+ but at the format level.

cards.q: What are the 4 ad formats?
cards.a: Flexible (AI chooses), Single image/video, Carousel (2+ swipe), Collection (hero + grid).

cards.q: When to use Collection vs Carousel?
cards.a: Collection: hero (video/image) + grid of products below. Ideal with a catalog, mobile only. Carousel: just a swipe of cards without a hero, ideal for storytelling without a catalog.

cards.q: What is Instant Experience?
cards.a: Full-screen post-click experience on mobile (formerly Canvas). Useful when your website is poor on mobile or you want to tell everything inside Meta.

cards.q: When is the Flexible format worthwhile?
cards.a: When you do NOT know which format performs best. Meta tests several and delivers the winner per person.

cards.q: On which devices do Collection and Instant Experience work?
cards.a: Mobile only (devices and placements). They don't exist on desktop.

cards.body: The placement is the exact spot where your ad shows up within Meta's universe: for example the Feed (the wall or home section you scroll with your finger), Stories (stories that last 24 hours), Reels (short vertical videos) or Marketplace (Facebook's buy-and-sell section). All these surfaces together are called Meta technologies (Meta's technologies: Facebook, Instagram, Messenger and the partner network). The key point the exam demands is this: not all placements accept the same formats or the same image proportions, so your piece must be designed thinking about where it will appear, not the other way around.

rows: Feeds, Stories, Reels, Marketplace, Right column, In-stream video
In-stream video, Native banner, Rewarded video (third-party apps)

cards.body: You have two ways to decide which placements your ad runs in. Advantage+ Placements (automatic placements, the default option) lets Meta's artificial intelligence distribute your ad where it works best, seeking maximum visibility (so more people see it) at the lowest cost. Manual placements is the opposite: you choose by hand where to appear, which gives granular control (fine, detailed). Manual is only justified in two cases: when you have a specific piece that performs better in a certain spot, or for brand safety (taking care of what content your ad runs next to). Example: La Parrilla Restaurant leaves Advantage+ Placements on because it doesn't know where its video performs best and wants Meta to figure it out on its own.

The aspect ratio (the proportion of the image: how wide it is against how tall, for example 9:16 vertical or 16:9 horizontal) decides whether your ad fills the screen or gets lost. Each placement has its own shape: a horizontal piece looks tiny and with black bars in Stories, while a vertical one wastes the sides in the Feed. Nailing the proportion is the difference between an ad that owns the phone screen and one the user ignores immediately. That's why many advertisers produce the same message in several proportions. Example: La Esquina Café films its promo in 9:16 for Reels and reframes a 1:1 version for the Feed.

cards.label: SAFE ZONE IN STORIES/REELS
cards.body: In Stories and Reels the UI (IG logo, profile, message button) covers the edges. If you put critical text near the border, it gets cut off. Design with the central "safe zone" of the 9:16 in mind.

The DentalCare clinic starts with Advantage+ Placements so Meta discovers where its ad performs best. Its Reels video has the clinic's phone number stuck right at the bottom edge, and that's a mistake: Instagram's message button falls right there and covers the number, so nobody can call. The solution isn't to change the audience or the budget, but to move that detail to the center of the 9:16 frame, inside the safe zone (the central strip the UI never covers). With that simple repositioning, the phone number is visible again and the calls come back.

cards.h: How this looks in Ads Manager (step by step)
cards.body: Placements are defined at the ad set (the second tier). The path is: Ads Manager → your Ad set → Placements section. There you see two buttons: Advantage+ placements (recommended and selected by default) and Manual placements. If you choose Manual, a list unfolds with checkboxes per platform (Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, Audience Network) and per surface (Feeds, Stories, Reels, etc.) that you can turn on or off one by one. The aspect ratio, on the other hand, isn't chosen here: it depends on the file you upload at the ad level. Example: DentalCare leaves Advantage+ placements on and uploads a 9:16 video with its phone number already centered.

cards.q: Optimal aspect ratio for Stories and Reels:
cards.explanation: 9:16 (full portrait vertical) takes the full screen in Stories and Reels.

cards.q: The full portrait aspect ratio that takes the full screen in Stories and Reels is ___.
cards.explanation: 9:16 = full portrait vertical. It fills the screen in Stories and Reels.

cards.q: A brand wants to maximize visibility by automating placements. Which setting?
options: Use only the FB Feed placement
cards.explanation: Advantage+ Placements automates. Manual gives control but usually performs worse on overall efficiency.

cards.q: The Right column and In-stream video placements belong to the ___ platform.
cards.explanation: Right column and In-stream video are Facebook placements. Threads only has Feeds.

cards.q: Why do you have to design the creative thinking about the safe zone in Stories/Reels?
options: The UI covers the edges and cuts off critical text
So the video file weighs less
cards.explanation: The Stories/Reels UI covers the edges. Critical text near the border gets cut off. Design within the central safe zone.

cards.q: When you need granular control for brand safety or specific creative, you use ___ placements.
cards.explanation: Manual placements give granular control. Advantage+ Placements automates but loses that control.

cards.q: Audience Network offers ads in:
cards.explanation: Audience Network extends ads to third-party apps: in-stream video, native banner and rewarded video.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: The contact number gets cut off in Reels
cards.body: Aceros del Bajío (a steel-profile SMB) uploads a video to Reels with the sales phone number stuck to the bottom edge. In testing they notice that Instagram's message button covers the number and nobody calls. The director thinks the problem is the audience.

cards.q: What is the real problem and the solution?
options: The audience is poorly targeted; redo it
The detail is outside the safe zone; move it to the center
The video is too heavy; compress it
Reels doesn't allow phone numbers inside the video
cards.explanation: The Stories/Reels UI (profile, message button) covers the edges. The critical detail must go in the central safe zone of the 9:16, not stuck to the border.

cards.q: They also want to use the same video in the Feed. What should they check?
options: The aspect ratio: 9:16 wastes space in the Feed
Use only the Right column placement
cards.explanation: Each placement has its own shape. 9:16 is optimal in Stories/Reels but in the Feed 4:5 or 1:1 is better. That's why creative is designed per placement.

cards.q: They don't know which placement the video performs best in. Which setting lets Meta decide?
options: Limit it to Audience Network only
cards.explanation: Advantage+ Placements automates and finds where the ad performs best. Manual only if you need specific control.

cards.q: Aspect ratio for Stories and Reels?
cards.a: 9:16 full portrait vertical (full screen).

cards.q: Recommended aspect ratio for the Feed?
cards.a: 4:5 (vertical) or 1:1 (square). 16:9 also in FB Feed/Marketplace.

cards.q: Advantage+ Placements vs Manual placements?
cards.a: Advantage+ (default): Meta automates, maximizes visibility/efficiency. Manual: granular control for brand safety or specific creative.

cards.q: What happens if your ad has critical text at the edges in Stories/Reels?
cards.a: It gets cut off. The UI (IG logo, profile, message button) covers the edges. Design for the central safe zone of the 9:16.

cards.q: What does Audience Network offer?
cards.a: Ads in third-party apps: in-stream video, native banner, rewarded video.

cards.body: Advantage+ Creative (creative powered by Meta's artificial intelligence) is a feature that takes your pieces and automatically adjusts them for each person who sees them and for each place they appear. The underlying idea is that there's no "single" perfect ad for everyone: what hooks one user in Reels may not work for another in the Feed. Instead of you making twenty versions by hand, the AI generates and distributes them on its own. It's the same spirit of Advantage+ you already saw in audiences and placements, but now applied to the visual content of the ad. That's why it's Meta's modern bet: you upload good raw material and let the system fine-tune it.

Breaking down what it does under the hood:
1. Variations (automatic variations): it generates different versions of your piece by changing the crop of the image, the brightness, the background music, etc., so as not to burn out a single version.
2. Enhancements (text and audio enhancements): it can translate the text, add captions or polish the sound so the piece communicates better.
3. Efficiency (smart delivery): of all the versions it generated, it shows each person the one most likely to work for them. Example: La Esquina Café uploads a single video of its latte; the AI tries a square crop for the Feed and a vertical one for Reels, and adds captions to the one that runs without sound.

cards.h: What Asset Customization for Placements is
cards.body: Asset Customization for Placements is the opposite of leaving everything on automatic: here you decide by hand which specific piece shows in each placement. For each location you can set things like the template (the assembly template), the media crop (how the image or video is cropped), the text per placement (a different text depending on the spot) and the priority of that version. It's useful when you already know that a certain piece performs better in a certain spot and you don't want to leave it to chance. The key rule the exam asks: if you use Asset Customization together with Advantage+ Creative, what you set by hand in that placement wins; the AI only rules where you didn't intervene. Example: La Esquina Café leaves Advantage+ Creative on for almost everything, but in Reels it sets its favorite vertical cut by hand, and that's the one that runs.

cards.h: What creative fatigue is and why to diversify
cards.body: Creative fatigue (also called "ad wear-out") is the performance drop that happens when the same people see the same ad too many times: they stop paying attention, ignore it and even block it. It's like a catchy song you end up hating from hearing it so much. The cure is to diversify: give the system several different pieces so it has fresh material to rotate and there's always something new to show. Changing one minor detail isn't enough; it's better to vary along three different fronts (which you'll see next). Example: Edu Premium runs the same video three months straight to the same audience and its cost per enrollment skyrockets; the problem isn't the algorithm, it's that the ad already tired everyone out.

rows: Headline, copy, CTA — vary the message
Image, video, format, color, person in the ad
Single image, video, carousel, collection — vary the container

cards.label: MOBILE-FIRST
cards.body:
Rules for mobile creative that performs:
• 9:16 video, ≤15 seconds
• Branding in the first 3 seconds
• Audio + captions (audio off is the default)
• Key elements in the central safe zone

Urban Store (youth clothing) uploads a video and leaves Advantage+ Creative on: the AI tries crops and captions per person. But for Reels it already has a specific vertical cut it knows works, so it pins it with Asset Customization. The result: in Reels its manual version wins; in the rest of the placements the AI rules. To avoid creative fatigue, it also rotates three different messages: a customer testimonial, an animation with the season's promo and a winter outfit. That way the system always has fresh material to deliver.

cards.h: How this looks in Ads Manager (step by step)
cards.body: This is configured at the ad level (the third tier, after campaign and ad set). The path is: Ads Manager → open your ad → Ad creative section. There, below the pieces you upload, the Advantage+ creative toggles appear, which are on by default; you can turn off specific enhancements if you don't want them. To pin a different piece per placement, you open the Customize by placement or Edit per placement option, and there you choose by hand which image or crop goes in Reels, in Stories, in the Feed, etc. Example: Urban Store goes to Ad creative, leaves Advantage+ creative on and then clicks "Customize by placement" only to replace the Reels video with its favorite vertical cut.

cards.q: If you use Advantage+ Creative AND Asset Customization together, which one prevails?
options: Asset Customization wins where you set it
They cancel each other out and neither applies
It depends on the placement randomly
cards.explanation: The manual setting wins. Asset Customization has priority over Advantage+ Creative in the placements where you explicitly customized.

cards.q: Mobile-first creative requires:
options: Putting the branding at the end of the video
cards.explanation: Mobile rules: 9:16, ≤15s, branding early, audio + captions, safe zone.

cards.q: A single repeated ad produces creative ___ (performance drops from overexposure).
cards.explanation: Creative fatigue = performance drop from overexposure. It's fought by diversifying the creative.

cards.q: The 3 dimensions to diversify the creative are messaging, visuals and ___.
cards.explanation: The 3 dimensions: messaging, visuals (image/video) and formats (container).

cards.q: Advantage+ Creative does 3 things. Which is NOT one of them?
options: Delivers the best variant to each person
cards.explanation: Advantage+ Creative optimizes the creative (variations, enhancements, efficiency per person). The budget is not a creative function.

cards.q: Branding in a mobile video should appear in the first ___ seconds.
cards.explanation: Branding in the first 3 seconds: the user decides quickly whether to keep watching.

cards.q: Audio on mobile by default is:
options: ON: it always plays with sound
OFF: that's why captions are needed
Active only in the Reels placement
Active only in the desktop placement
cards.explanation: Audio off is the default on mobile. That's why the creative needs captions to communicate without sound.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: Edu Premium's CPA rises every month
cards.body: Edu Premium runs the same video all quarter to the same audience. The first month the cost per enrollment is low; by the third month it skyrockets and the director blames the algorithm. You detect the real cause.

cards.q: What is happening and how is it solved?
options: The algorithm failed; everything must be paused
Creative fatigue; diversify message and format
The audience is too small; increase the budget
The Pixel stopped working; reinstall it
cards.explanation: A single repeated creative produces creative fatigue: performance drops from overexposure. It's fought by rotating message, visuals and formats.

cards.q: Urban Store wants the AI to adapt the creative per person and placement, but in Reels it already has a cut that works. How does it configure this?
options: Only Advantage+ Creative, ignoring the cut
Advantage+ Creative ON + Asset Customization in Reels
cards.explanation: Advantage+ Creative optimizes per person; Asset Customization pins the manual setting in the placement where there's already a winner. In Reels the manual wins; in the rest, the AI.

cards.q: Urban Store's new Reels video is 30s with the logo at the end. What do you fix?
options: Nothing: the video is fine as is
Shorten to ≤15s and put branding in the first 3s
Extend it to 60s to tell more
Remove the captions so it weighs less
cards.explanation: Mobile-first: ≤15s, branding in the first 3s, audio + captions (audio off is the default), key elements in the safe zone.

cards.q: What does Advantage+ Creative do?
cards.a: AI that optimizes creative per viewer and placement: generates automatic variations, text/audio enhancements and delivers the best variant to each person.

cards.q: What happens if you combine Advantage+ Creative with Asset Customization?
cards.a: Asset Customization (manual) has priority in the placements where you set it. Advantage+ Creative applies in the rest.

cards.q: Mobile-first creative rules?
cards.a: 9:16 video, ≤15 seconds, branding in the first 3s, audio + captions, key elements in the central safe zone.

cards.q: What is creative fatigue and how is it fought?
cards.a: Performance drop from overexposure of the same ad. It's fought by diversifying in messaging, visuals and formats.

cards.q: The 3 dimensions of creative diversification:
cards.a: Messaging (headline/copy/CTA), Visuals (image/video/format/color) and Formats (single/video/carousel/collection).

cards.h: What a KPI is and why it matters
cards.body: A KPI (Key Performance Indicator) is the number that really tells you whether your campaign is working, separating it from the pile of secondary data. Not all figures are worth the same: many are noise. The KPI is the one the boss looks at to decide whether to keep investing or shut it off. The reason the exam insists on this is that each campaign objective has its own KPI, and looking at the wrong one leads you to false conclusions. Here you learn what the two universal KPIs are and then how they change by funnel stage.

There are two numbers that appear in any report and apply regardless of the objective:
1. Results: the number of times exactly what you set as your goal was achieved. If your objective was purchases, Results are the purchases; if it was leads (prospects who leave their info), they're the leads; if it was video views, they're the views.
2. Cost per Result: how much each of those wins cost you. The formula is Amount spent ÷ Results. Example: if La Esquina Café spent 2,000 pesos and got 100 delivery orders, its Cost per Result is 20 pesos per order. That figure tells you at a glance whether the business is profitable.

rows: Reach, Frequency, Estimated ad recall lift, Impressions, CPM
Link clicks, CTR, Landing page views, Cost per landing page view, Conversations started
Purchases, Purchase ROAS, Leads, Adds to cart, Cost per purchase

cards.body: Attribution is the process of deciding which ad gets the credit when someone buys. It sounds obvious, but it isn't: before buying, a person usually has several touchpoints (every time they see or tap your brand, e.g. they see an ad on Instagram on Monday, another on Facebook on Tuesday and buy on Wednesday). That whole journey is called the customer journey (the customer's path from discovering you to buying). Attribution splits the credit among those touchpoints. Without it, you wouldn't know which ad to shut off and which to scale, because you don't know which one actually brought the sale. Example: someone saw La Esquina Café's ad three times before ordering; attribution decides whether the sale counts for that campaign.

cards.h: The 2 attribution models in Ads Manager
cards.body:
In Ads Manager there are only two attribution models; the exam asks this often to trip you up with names that don't exist.
1. Standard attribution: it splits the credit following fixed rules (a time window and a touchpoint type). It counts impressions (the times the ad was shown), clicks and video plays. It's correlational: it says "this sale happened after seeing the ad".
2. Incremental attribution: it's a predictive model that tries to answer the hard question: "did the ad cause the sale, or was that person going to buy anyway?". It's more demanding and measures real causality.
HEADS UP: "Custom attribution" does NOT exist in Ads Manager. If they mention it, it's a trap.

cards.h: What the attribution setting (the window) is
cards.body: The attribution setting is the time window within which a sale still "counts" for the ad. By default Meta uses 1-day view, 7-day click. Translate it like this: if the person only viewed the ad (without tapping it) and buys within 1 day, it's attributed; if they clicked the ad, they have up to 7 days to buy and it still counts. Past that period, the sale is no longer credited to that ad. Memorize that pair of numbers literally because the exam asks it exactly. Example: a customer clicked La Esquina Café's ad on Monday and ordered on Saturday (6 days later): that falls within the 7-day click window, so it is attributed.

cards.label: EXAM TRAP
cards.body: If they ask you about "Custom attribution" or "Engaged-view attribution" in Ads Manager, they're traps. Only Standard and Incremental exist. Custom does NOT exist.

A prospect saw the real-estate firm Casa Plus's ad on Monday, didn't click, and requested a quote on Tuesday. With the default window (1-day view) that quote IS attributed to the ad, because they acted within the day after viewing it. If they had requested it 5 days later without clicking, it wouldn't count: the view window is just one day. And to judge the campaign, Casa Plus looks at the right KPI for the objective: if it's a brand campaign, it looks at Reach and recall; if it's a quotes campaign, it looks at Leads and cost per purchase (cost per quote). Looking at the wrong number would lead it to cancel a campaign that actually worked.

cards.h: How this looks in Ads Manager (step by step)
cards.body: Both the model and the window are adjusted at the ad set (second tier) and when reporting. The path for the window is: Ads Manager → your Ad set → Attribution setting section, where you choose the pair (for example the default 1-day view, 7-day click). To read results with one model or the other, you go to Ads Manager → Columns → Comparing attribution settings or the Compare attribution settings menu, and there you switch between Standard and Incremental. What you'll never find is a "Custom" option: it doesn't exist. Example: Casa Plus leaves the default window in its Ad set and, at month's end, compares with Incremental to see how many quotes the campaign actually caused.

cards.q: What are the 2 attribution models available in Ads Manager?
cards.explanation: Only Standard and Incremental. Custom doesn't exist in Ads Manager. Last-click is a behavior within Standard.

cards.q: The default attribution setting in Ads Manager is 1-day view, ___-day click.
cards.explanation: Default = 1-day view, 7-day click. Memorize it literally: the exam asks it that way.

cards.q: Which are Awareness metrics? Select 3.
cards.explanation: Reach, Frequency and Estimated ad recall lift are Awareness metrics. ROAS and cost per purchase are Conversion metrics.

cards.q: Cost per Result is calculated as Amount spent divided by ___.
cards.explanation: Cost per Result = Amount spent / Results. Results = times the performance goal was achieved.

cards.q: Standard attribution differs from Incremental in that:
options: Standard uses rules; Incremental models causality
Standard measures sales; Incremental measures recall
They are exactly the same, different name
Incremental doesn't exist within Ads Manager
cards.explanation: Standard attributes with rules (correlational). Incremental is predictive: it identifies whether the ad actually CAUSED the conversion.

cards.q: Purchase ROAS and Cost per purchase are metrics from the ___ stage.
cards.explanation: Conversion: Purchases, Purchase ROAS, Leads, Adds to cart, Cost per purchase. Reach/Frequency are Awareness metrics.

cards.q: They ask you about 'Custom attribution' in Ads Manager. What do you answer?
options: That it's the default attribution model
It's a trap: there's only Standard and Incremental
That it's exactly the same as Incremental
That it only applies to Awareness campaigns
cards.explanation: Custom attribution does NOT exist in Ads Manager. Only Standard and Incremental. It's a typical exam trap.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: The director looks at the wrong metric
cards.body: Aceros del Bajío runs a pre-expo Awareness campaign and the director judges it by the number of quotes (Leads), concludes it "didn't work" and wants to cancel it. He also thinks a sale at 5 days without a click should be attributed to the ad.

cards.q: Why is the director evaluating the Awareness campaign wrong?
options: He's right: Awareness is measured by Leads
Awareness is measured by Reach and recall, not Leads
You have to wait longer to evaluate it
cards.explanation: Looking at the wrong funnel metric leads to false conclusions. Awareness = Reach/Frequency/recall. Leads is a Conversion metric.

cards.q: A prospect saw the ad, did NOT click, and requested a quote 5 days later. With the default window, is it attributed to the ad?
options: Yes: the ad always gets the conversion attributed
No: the view window is 1 day; it fell outside
Yes, because there was an implicit click on viewing
It depends on the campaign budget
cards.explanation: Default = 1-day view, 7-day click. Viewed without a click → it only counts if they bought within 1 day. At 5 days without a click it falls outside.

cards.q: The director wants to know whether the ad REALLY caused the conversions, not just whether it correlated. Which attribution model?
cards.explanation: Incremental attribution is the predictive model that identifies causal conversions. Standard only attributes with rules (correlational).

cards.q: The 2 main KPI metrics?
cards.a: Results (achievements of the performance goal) and Cost per Result (Amount spent / Results).

cards.q: Awareness metrics:
cards.a: Reach, Frequency, Estimated ad recall lift, Impressions, CPM.

cards.q: Conversion metrics:
cards.a: Purchases, Purchase ROAS, Leads, Adds to cart, Cost per purchase.

cards.q: What are the 2 attribution models in Ads Manager?
cards.a: Standard (with rules, correlational) and Incremental (predictive causal modeling). Custom does NOT exist.

cards.q: Default attribution setting?
cards.a: 1-day view, 7-day click.

cards.q: Difference between Standard and Incremental attribution?
cards.a: Standard = attributes with rules (window + touchpoint), correlational. Incremental = predictive causality model.

lessons.title: Explainer · A/B test, Brand lift and Conversion Lift

cards.body: An A/B test (also called a split test) is a controlled experiment: you run two versions of an ad at the same time and let them compete to see which works better. The golden rule is to change a single variable between the two versions — it can be the creative (the ad's image or video), the audience, the placement (the spot where it shows, e.g. feed vs Stories) or the delivery — and keep everything else identical. Why only one? Because if you change two things at once and one version wins, you'd never know which of the two changes was responsible.

cards.h: How the A/B test splits people
cards.body: For the comparison to be fair, Meta splits your audience randomly and without overlap (that is, the same person never sees both versions; so their decision isn't "contaminated"). In the end, the winner is the version with the lowest cost per result (what each purchase, lead or click cost you, depending on your objective). Unlike the other two experiments in this lesson, the A/B test doesn't require a minimum spend, and it's recommended to let it run at least 7 days (ideally 14 to 30) so it gathers enough data.

cards.label: A/B TEST RULE
cards.body: One single variable at a time. If you change the creative and the audience together, you can no longer say which one caused the difference. Let Meta split the audience automatically (without overlap) and give it time: minimum 7 days, ideally 14-30.

cards.h: Brand lift: measures perception, not sales
cards.body: Brand lift measures how much your campaign changed what people think and remember about your brand — not how much you sold. It works with surveys: Meta asks a group that DID see your ads (the test group or exposed group) and a similar group that did NOT see them (the control group), and then compares the responses of both. The difference between the two groups is the "lift", that is, the boost your ads gave.

1. Brand awareness: whether people know your brand exists. 2. Ad recall: whether people remember having seen your ad in recent days. 3. Message association: whether they connect your brand with the idea you wanted to convey (e.g. associate "Volaris" with "cheap flights"). Since everything depends on gathering enough survey responses to be reliable, Brand lift does require a minimum budget, which varies by region.

cards.h: Conversion Lift: measures EXTRA sales caused
cards.body: Conversion Lift measures something different: how many extra conversions (sales, leads or app events) your campaign generated that would not have happened without it. This is called incrementality: it separates the sales the ad really caused from the ones that were going to happen anyway. To do this it uses an RCT (Randomized Control Trial): it randomly splits people between a group that sees the ads and a control group that doesn't, and measures the real difference in sales between them. It answers the boss's question: "how many extra sales did this campaign bring me?". Meta recommends feeding it with Conversions API (CAPI) data so the measurement is more complete.

cards.h: Where they're configured in the platform
cards.body: The three experiments — A/B test, Brand lift and Conversion Lift — live in the same section: Experiments, which you find inside Ads Manager or Meta Business Suite. Heads up: they're not built or read in regular Ads Reporting (the usual metrics report). And another practical rule: each test runs in a dedicated campaign created for the experiment; don't touch campaigns that are already running, because that would dirty the results.

cards.label: TYPICAL TRAP
cards.body: Confusing Brand lift with Conversion Lift. Brand lift = surveys, measures perception/brand. Conversion Lift = observation of real sales/leads. Exam trick: if the question mentions "how many extra sales?" → Conversion Lift. If it mentions "brand impact / recall" → Brand lift.

Hotel Lumière sponsors a food event. Question 1: do travelers remember the brand after seeing the ads? → since it's perception, you use Brand lift (test vs control surveys). Question 2, a quarter later: how many extra bookings did the campaign generate versus not having run it? → since it's sales incrementality, you use Conversion Lift. Question 3: between two videos that only change the first scene, which one converts more cheaply? → since you're comparing a single variable, you use an A/B test.

cards.h: How this looks in Ads Manager (step by step)
cards.body: Imagine you're going to build the test of Hotel Lumière's two videos: you go to Ads Manager → Experiments → A/B test, choose the variable to test ("Creative"), upload Video A and Video B, set the budget and the days (say 14), and hit run. Meta splits the audience without overlap and, when it finishes, marks the winner by lowest cost per result. That same Experiments menu is where you'd build the Brand lift (it will ask you for the minimum budget and set up the surveys) or the Conversion Lift (it will let you connect your Pixel/CAPI as the source of the sales).

cards.q: You want to know how many EXTRA sales your campaign generated vs not having run it. Which test?
cards.explanation: Conversion Lift measures incrementality: causal sales attributable to the campaign vs the control that didn't see it.

cards.q: In a correct A/B test, how many variables should you change?
cards.explanation: ONE single variable. If you change 2+, you can't isolate which one caused the result.

cards.q: Brand lift measures brand impact using ___ on a test group vs a control group.
cards.explanation: Brand lift uses surveys (ad recall, awareness, message association). It's perception, not sales.

cards.q: Conversion Lift measures ___ (causal) conversions attributable to the campaign.
cards.explanation: Conversion Lift measures incremental conversions: the EXTRA sales the campaign caused vs the control.

cards.q: Where are A/B test, Brand lift and Conversion Lift configured?
options: In Experiments (Ads Manager or Business Suite)
cards.explanation: All 3 live in Experiments, in dedicated campaigns. NOT in regular Ads Reporting.

cards.q: The A/B test splits the audience randomly without ___ so as not to contaminate the result.
cards.explanation: Without overlap: if the same people see both ads, you can't know which one caused the conversion. Meta splits it automatically.

cards.q: You want to know whether a fair campaign improved brand recall. Which test?
options: An A/B test between two creatives
cards.explanation: Brand lift measures brand impact with surveys. Recall is a classic Brand lift metric.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.body: Aceros del Bajío (B2B, steel profiles) ran a 3-week brand campaign before an expo and then a quotes campaign. The director asks: do people remember the brand? how many extra quotes did the campaign generate? and which headline works better between two versions?

cards.q: To find out whether the industrial buyers REMEMBER the brand, which tool?
options: The Standard reporting of Ads Manager
cards.explanation: Recall/perception → Brand lift: surveys to a test group (exposed) vs a control group (not exposed). It doesn't measure sales.

cards.q: To find out how many EXTRA quotes the campaign caused vs not having run it, which tool?
options: An A/B test between two variants
The Standard reporting of Ads Manager
cards.explanation: Extra causal quotes = incrementality → Conversion Lift. Recommended source: Conversions API.

cards.q: To compare 2 versions of the same ad changing only the headline, which tool?
options: A/B test (a single variable: the headline)
The Standard reporting of Ads Manager
cards.explanation: Comparing 2 variants changing ONE single variable (the headline) is exactly an A/B test. All 3 tests live in Experiments.

cards.q: Difference between A/B test, Brand lift and Conversion Lift?
cards.a: A/B test = compares 2 variants (1 variable). Brand lift = surveys about brand/recall. Conversion Lift = measures extra causal sales.

cards.q: How many variables are changed in an A/B test?
cards.a: ONE only. If you change 2+ you can't isolate the cause. Without overlap (Meta splits automatically).

cards.q: What does Brand lift measure and how?
cards.a: It measures brand awareness, ad recall and message association with surveys to a test group vs a control group. It's perception, not sales.

cards.q: What does Conversion Lift measure and how?
cards.a: Incremental conversions (causal sales/leads) with randomized control trials. Recommended source: Conversions API.

cards.q: Where do A/B test, Brand lift, Conversion Lift run?
cards.a: In Experiments (Ads Manager or Meta Business Suite), in dedicated campaigns. NOT in Ads Reporting.

cards.q: Does the A/B test have a minimum spend? And Brand lift?
cards.a: A/B test does NOT have a minimum spend. Brand lift DOES have budget minimums by region.

lessons.title: Explainer · Brand Safety and Advertising Standards

cards.h: What Brand Safety is and what it solves
cards.body: Brand Safety is the set of controls that decide next to what content your ad can appear. The problem it solves is real: your ad is distributed across many surfaces and other people's videos, and you don't want your brand to show up next to something violent, controversial or of bad reputation, because people associate the two things. Imagine paying for a nice diaper ad that ends up next to an aggressive video: the damage to the image is immediate. Meta offers four controls, ranging from the simplest and most automatic to the most manual and specific. As you go down the list you gain precision but also more configuration work.

rows: Expanded (default) / Moderate / Limited — control which inventory is accepted
Exclude specific publishers where you do NOT want to appear

cards.h: The 4 Brand Safety controls broken down
cards.body:
From simplest to most manual:
1. Inventory filters: a three-level switch — Expanded (broad, the default and widest reach), Moderate and Limited (the most restrictive) — that regulates how sensitive neighboring content can be. It's the easiest control because it's a single choice.
2. Publisher block lists: you build by hand a list of specific sites or accounts where you do NOT want to appear.
3. Topic exclusions: you turn off entire topics, such as controversial news or certain genres.
4. Content type exclusions: you exclude whole formats, e.g. in-stream pre-roll (the ads that run before a video). Example: the PetCare veterinary clinic raises its inventory filter to Moderate and, additionally, puts a specific site it dislikes in its block list.

The Advertising Standards are Meta's rules about what you can and can't advertise; they're different from Brand Safety. Brand Safety takes care of what your ad runs next to; the Advertising Standards take care of the content of your own ad. There are two categories: content that's entirely prohibited and content that's restricted (allowed only under certain conditions). The exam uses them for trick questions, so it's worth recognizing them. Example: a financial firm can advertise, but only if it meets requirements; on the other hand, violent content is prohibited without exception.

Review the typical cases:
1. Entirely prohibited: tobacco (tobacco and cigarettes), privacy violations (violating people's privacy), violent/graphic content, deceptive practices (e.g. promising something false), drugs (recreational drugs).
2. Restricted (allowed with conditions): alcohol (with geographic restriction, depending on the country), financial services (allowed with a disclosure — a clear legal notice — and an account older than 90 days), online gambling (only with prior authorization from Meta).
3. Personal attributes: you can't imply a personal characteristic such as age, weight, race or religion, e.g. "lose weight easily" or "for older gentlemen". This takes down ads even if the product is completely legal. Example: a clinic uses "for experienced professionals" instead of "for older gentlemen" and so it doesn't get rejected.

cards.label: TYPICAL TRAP
cards.body: An ad can be rejected for personal attributes even if the product is legal. E.g. "for older gentlemen" can trigger the rule against implying personal characteristics. Better: "for experienced professionals".

The PetCare veterinary clinic doesn't want its ads to run next to controversial news. The simplest thing is to raise the inventory filter to Moderate or Limited, because with a single choice it reduces the risk everywhere. If it also dislikes one site in particular, it puts it in a publisher block list to exclude it by hand. And it takes care of the copy (the ad's text): it writes "for responsible owners" instead of something that implies the person's age, and so it avoids being rejected for personal attributes. None of these fixes have anything to do with the audience or the budget: they're brand-safety and compliance adjustments.

cards.h: How this looks in Ads Manager (step by step)
cards.body: The Brand Safety controls live at the ad set (second tier) and at the account level. The path is: Ads Manager → your Ad set → Brand safety and suitability section. There you choose the inventory filter (Expanded / Moderate / Limited) and, below, apply block lists, topic exclusions and content type exclusions. The block lists are also managed centrally in Business Settings → Brand safety to reuse them across several campaigns. The Advertising Standards, on the other hand, are not "turned on": they're rules Meta reviews when approving your ad, so the work there is to write the copy well. Example: PetCare goes to Brand safety and suitability, sets the filter to Moderate and adds the site it doesn't want to its block list.

cards.q: Which is NOT a Brand Safety control?
cards.explanation: Bid Cap is a bid strategy, not brand safety. Inventory filter, publisher block list and topic exclusions are 3 of the brand safety controls + content type exclusions.

cards.q: The simplest and widest-reaching Brand Safety control is the ___.
cards.explanation: The inventory filters (Expanded/Moderate/Limited) are the simplest control. Block lists require building the list by hand.

cards.q: The default inventory filter is ___.
cards.explanation: Expanded is the default (widest reach). Moderate and Limited restrict the inventory more.

cards.q: An ad for a legal product is rejected. The likely cause:
options: The campaign budget is too low
Personal attributes: the copy implies a personal trait
The site Pixel is not installed
The campaign audience is too large
cards.explanation: Personal attributes: you can't imply the user's age, weight, race or religion. Reword the copy neutrally.

cards.q: To exclude specific publishers where you do NOT want to appear you use publisher ___ lists.
cards.explanation: Publisher block lists exclude specific publishers. They have to be built by hand, which is why they're less simple than the inventory filter.

cards.q: PetCare doesn't want to appear next to controversial news. Which is the simplest control?
options: Build a manual publisher block list of each site
Raise the inventory filter to Moderate or Limited
cards.explanation: The inventory filter (Moderate/Limited) is the simplest and widest-reaching control. Block lists and topic exclusions are more manual.

cards.q: Financial services can advertise if:
options: Only if they advertise on desktop
Only if the account has a catalog
cards.explanation: Financial services are restricted but allowed with proper disclosure (>90 days). It's restricted content, not an absolute prohibition.

cards.label: CASE TO SOLVE
cards.h: PetCare · a brand cared for responsibly
cards.body: PetCare sells to families and cares about its image. Its ad says "ideal for older gentlemen who want a dog" and was rejected. Also, the director doesn't want the brand to appear next to controversial news and asks about the simplest way to avoid it.

cards.q: Why was the ad rejected and how is it corrected?
options: Because of the low budget; it has to be raised
Because of personal attributes (age); reword the copy
Because of using video; change the ad to an image
Because of the small audience; it has to be widened
cards.explanation: "For older gentlemen" implies a personal characteristic (age). Reword it neutrally even though the product is legal.

cards.q: To avoid appearing next to controversial news in the SIMPLEST way, what does it do?
options: Build a publisher block list by hand
Raise the inventory filter to Moderate
Turn off Advantage+ Placements on the set
cards.explanation: The inventory filter (Moderate/Limited) is the simplest and widest-reaching control. The block list is more manual.

cards.q: If it also wants to exclude a specific publisher site it dislikes, which control does it use?
cards.explanation: A publisher block list excludes specific publishers. The inventory filter is broader; block lists are for specific sites.

cards.q: What are the 4 brand safety controls?
cards.a: Inventory filters (Expanded/Moderate/Limited), Publisher block lists, Topic exclusions, Content type exclusions.

cards.q: What is the simplest Brand Safety control?
cards.a: The inventory filter (Expanded default / Moderate / Limited). Wider reach and less manual work than block lists.

cards.q: Which Advertising Standards prohibit or restrict content?
cards.a: Tobacco, privacy violations, violent/graphic, deceptive practices, alcohol (geo), drugs, online gambling without authorization, personal attributes.

cards.q: Why can an ad be rejected for 'personal attributes' even if the product is legal?
cards.a: Because it implies a personal characteristic of the user (age, weight, race, religion). Reword: "for experienced professionals" instead of "for older gentlemen".

cards.q: Is Bid Cap a Brand Safety control?
cards.a: No. Bid Cap is a bid strategy. The Brand Safety controls are inventory filters, publisher block lists, topic exclusions and content type exclusions.

lessons.title: Integrative case · measuring a B2B campaign

cards.label: REAL SCENARIO
cards.body: Aceros del Bajío sells steel profiles to construction companies. It ran a 3-week brand campaign before an expo and now a quotes campaign. The director asks: do people remember the brand? and how many extra quotes did the campaign generate? Let's solve it together, step by step.
They are two different questions and each has its own test. "Do they remember the brand?" is perception. "How many extra quotes?" is incrementality. Don't mix them in a single report.

To measure whether they remember the brand you use Brand lift: surveys to a test group (saw the ad) vs a control group (didn't see it). Key metric: ad recall. NOT Conversion Lift, which measures sales, not perception.
To find out how many quotes the campaign caused (not just correlated) you use Conversion Lift: it compares test vs control to isolate the incremental conversions. Recommended source: Conversions API.
Both tests live in Experiments (Ads Manager or Business Suite), in dedicated campaigns — not in regular Ads Reporting and without touching the campaigns that are already running.

cards.label: THE REASONING
cards.body: Notice the pattern: is the question about perception or causal sales? Perception/recall → Brand lift. Extra sales/incrementality → Conversion Lift. Comparing variants → A/B test. That single question solves almost any exam case.

cards.q: Aceros del Bajío now wants to compare 2 versions of the same ad changing only the headline. Which tool?
cards.explanation: Comparing 2 variants changing a single variable (the headline) is exactly an A/B test. Brand lift measures recall, Conversion Lift measures causal sales.

lessons.title: Match the key concepts of Day 6
lessons.instruction: Tap the term and then its definition. Each mistake costs 1 ❤️.
pairs: Compare 2 versions of a creative with the same audience (1 variable)
Measure impact on recall/awareness with surveys
Measure how many extra (causal) sales the campaign generated
Attribute conversions with rules (1d view, 7d click)
Hero (video/image) + grid of catalog products, mobile only
Full portrait aspect ratio of Stories and Reels
AI that optimizes variations/enhancements per person and placement
Simplest Brand Safety control: Expanded, Moderate, Limited
lessons.explanation: Each tool answers a different question. A/B test compares variants. Brand lift measures brand. Conversion Lift measures causal sales. Attribution assigns credit. Collection and 9:16 are creative; inventory filters is brand safety.

lessons.title: Order the results analysis (6 steps)
lessons.instruction: The official PDF defines 6 steps to analyze campaign results. Reorder with ▲▼.
items: Review Results (main KPI of the performance goal)
Review other metrics (cost per result, ROAS, CTR, frequency, etc.)
Compare ad sets against each other (identify winners and losers)
Identify optimization opportunities (what to change for the next period)
lessons.explanation: Correct flow: KPI first (did it meet the objective?) → secondary metrics → setup (how it was achieved) → holistic evaluation → internal comparison → next steps. Skipping steps leads to wrong conclusions.

cards.body: When a campaign ends (or closes its period), Meta defines in the official PDF a process of 6 steps in a fixed order to analyze results. The logic is funnel-shaped: first you understand what was achieved, then how it was achieved, and at the end you decide what to change. The exam asks this order directly, so we'll go through each step calmly — one per screen — and at the end you assemble the sequence.

It all starts with the main KPI of the performance goal (the metric you promised to optimize: purchases, leads, installs…). The question is just one: did the campaign meet its objective, yes or no? No getting distracted by clicks or impressions yet. Example: Hotel Lumière ran a campaign with a bookings goal; step 1 is to see how many bookings it brought: 42.

With the main result in hand, you give it context with the secondary metrics: cost per result, ROAS (return on ad spend), CTR (click-through rate) and frequency (times each person saw the ad). These tell you whether the result was cheap or expensive, and whether the ad connected or tired out the audience. Hotel Lumière: the 42 bookings cost $180 each with a frequency of 2.1 — healthy.

Now you review how the campaign was configured: the creative (image/video), the budget (budget and its distribution), the audience and the placements. This step connects the numbers with the decisions that produced them. Hotel Lumière: it discovers that 80% of the bookings came from the pool video in Reels, with Advantage+ Audience.

Here you put the three pieces together: results + metrics + setup. An isolated metric deceives — a high ROAS with a frequency of 9 is a campaign about to burn out; a high cost with a winning creative is an audience problem, not an ad problem. The holistic evaluation is what separates the media buyer from the one who just reads dashboards. Hotel Lumière: good cost + good creative + broad audience = the recipe worked completely.

cards.h: Step 5 — Compare ad sets against each other
cards.body: Within the same campaign, you compare the ad sets against one another to identify winners and losers: which audience, creative or placement performed best? This internal comparison is the learning gold mine, because it controls all external variables (same brand, same dates, same objective). Hotel Lumière: the couples ad set produced bookings at $150; the families one, at $260.

The close of the flow: with everything above, you define what to change for the next period — scale the winning ad set, pause the losing one, refresh the tired creative, adjust the budget. Analysis that doesn't end in decisions is just contemplation. Hotel Lumière: it doubles the budget on the couples ad set and tests a new video for families.

cards.label: EXAM TRICK
cards.body: Memorize the order as WHAT → HOW → WHAT'S NEXT: steps 1-2 measure what was achieved (result and cost), steps 3-4 explain how (setup and joint evaluation), steps 5-6 decide what's next (compare and optimize). The typical exam distractor puts 'compare ad sets' before 'review results' — whoever doesn't remember that the main KPI ALWAYS goes first falls for it.

cards.q: The format with a big hero on top + a grid of catalog products below is ___.
cards.explanation: Collection = hero + grid of catalog products (mobile only). Carousel is a swipe without a hero.

cards.q: The full portrait aspect ratio for Stories and Reels is ___.
cards.explanation: 9:16 takes the full screen in Stories and Reels. Design within the central safe zone.

cards.q: In Ads Manager there are only 2 attribution models: Standard and ___.
cards.explanation: Only Standard and Incremental. Custom does NOT exist in Ads Manager — it's a typical trap.

cards.q: A correct A/B test changes ___ variable at a time.
cards.explanation: ONE single variable. If you change 2+, you can't isolate which one caused the result.

cards.q: The test that measures how many EXTRA (causal) sales the campaign generated is ___ Lift.
cards.explanation: Conversion Lift measures incrementality (causal sales). Brand lift measures recall/perception with surveys.

cards.q: The 3 tests (A/B, Brand lift, Conversion Lift) are configured in ___.
cards.explanation: All 3 live in Experiments (Ads Manager or Business Suite), in dedicated campaigns.

cards.q: The simplest and widest-reaching Brand Safety control is the ___.
cards.explanation: The inventory filters (Expanded/Moderate/Limited) are the simplest control. Block lists require building the list by hand.

cards.q: An ad can be rejected for implying personal ___ even if the product is legal.
cards.explanation: Personal attributes: you can't imply the user's age, weight, race or religion. Reword the copy neutrally.

cards.q: Aspect ratio for Stories and Reels:
cards.explanation: 9:16 full portrait.

cards.q: Brand lift measures:
options: The ad's bid in the auction
cards.explanation: Brand lift = surveys about the brand.

cards.q: Conversion Lift measures:
options: The efficiency of the assigned budget
cards.explanation: Conversion Lift = extra causal sales.

cards.q: Attribution models in Ads Manager:
cards.explanation: Only Standard and Incremental. Custom does NOT exist.

cards.q: Default attribution setting:
cards.explanation: 1d view + 7d click is the default.

cards.q: An A/B test requires changing:
cards.explanation: Only 1 variable. If you change 2+ you can't isolate the cause.

cards.q: Format with hero + product grid:
cards.explanation: Collection = hero + catalog grid.

cards.q: Brand Safety controls (does NOT include):
cards.explanation: Bid Cap is a bid strategy. The first 3 are brand safety.

cards.q: If Advantage+ Creative and Asset Customization are both turned on:
options: Asset Customization has priority where it applies
They cancel out and neither applies
It only applies in Sales campaigns
cards.explanation: The manual setting wins. Asset Customization has priority.

cards.q: Where do you run A/B test, Brand lift and Conversion Lift?
options: In Experiments (Ads Manager or Business Suite)
cards.explanation: Experiments tool.

cards.q: Mobile-first creative requires:
options: Putting the branding at the end of the video
cards.explanation: Mobile rules: 9:16, ≤15s, branding early, audio + captions, safe zone.

cards.q: Which are Awareness metrics? Select 3.
cards.explanation: Reach, Frequency and Estimated ad recall lift are Awareness metrics. ROAS and cost per purchase are Conversion metrics.

lessons.title: Lightning — 10 quick questions
lessons.instruction: 10 quick questions — intensive review of creative & measurement. 4 minutes.

options: Manual wins in the customized placements

questions.explanation: Bid Cap is a bid strategy.

cards.q: What are the 4 ad formats?
cards.a: Flexible (AI chooses), Single image/video, Carousel (2+ swipe), Collection (hero + grid).

cards.q: When to use Collection vs Carousel?
cards.a: Collection: hero (video/image) + grid of products below. Ideal with a catalog. Carousel: just a swipe of cards without a hero, ideal for storytelling without a catalog.

cards.q: Aspect ratio for Stories and Reels?
cards.a: 9:16 full portrait vertical.

cards.q: What is Instant Experience?
cards.a: Full-screen post-click experience on mobile. Useful when your website is poor or you want to tell everything inside Meta.

cards.q: What are the 2 attribution models in Ads Manager?
cards.a: Standard (with rules) and Incremental (causal modeling). Custom does NOT exist.

cards.q: Default attribution setting?
cards.a: 1-day view, 7-day click.

cards.q: Difference between A/B test, Brand lift and Conversion Lift?
cards.a: A/B test = compares 2 variants (1 variable). Brand lift = surveys about brand/recall. Conversion Lift = measures extra causal sales.

cards.q: How many variables are changed in an A/B test?
cards.a: ONE only. If you change 2+ you can't isolate the cause.

cards.q: Where do A/B test, Brand lift, Conversion Lift run?
cards.a: In Experiments (Ads Manager or Meta Business Suite). NOT in Ads Reporting.

cards.q: Mobile-first creative rules?
cards.a: 9:16 video, ≤15 seconds, branding in the first 3s, audio + captions, key elements in the central safe zone.

cards.q: What are the 4 brand safety controls?
cards.a: Inventory filters (Expanded/Moderate/Limited), Publisher block lists, Topic exclusions, Content type exclusions.

cards.q: What happens if you combine Advantage+ Creative with Asset Customization?
cards.a: Asset Customization (manual) has priority in the placements where you set it. Advantage+ Creative applies in the other placements.

cards.q: The 2 main KPI metrics?
cards.a: Results (achievements of the performance goal) and Cost per Result (spent/results).

cards.q: Awareness metrics:
cards.a: Reach, Frequency, Estimated ad recall lift, Impressions, CPM.

cards.q: Conversion metrics:
cards.a: Purchases, Purchase ROAS, Leads, Adds to cart, Cost per purchase, Conversion rate.

cards.q: Which Advertising Standards prohibit content?
cards.a: Tobacco, privacy violations, violent/graphic, deceptive practices, drugs, online gambling without authorization, personal attributes ("for older gentlemen").

cards.q: Why can an ad be rejected for 'personal attributes' even if the product is legal?
cards.a: Because it implies a personal characteristic of the user (age, weight, race, religion). Reword the copy: "for experienced professionals" instead of "for older gentlemen".

cards.q: Difference between Standard and Incremental attribution?
cards.a: Standard = attributes with rules (window + touchpoint), correlational. Incremental = predictive causality model ("did the ad really cause the sale?").

cards.q: What happens if your ad creative has critical text at the edges in Stories?
cards.a: It gets cut off. The Stories UI (IG logo, profile, button) covers the edges. Design for the central safe zone.

cards.q: The 6 steps to analyze campaign results:
cards.a: 1) Review Results · 2) Other metrics · 3) Setup strategies · 4) Holistic evaluation · 5) Compare ad sets · 6) Identify optimization opportunities.

cards.q: What does Advantage+ Creative do?
cards.a: AI that optimizes creative per viewer and placement: automatic variations, text/audio enhancements and delivers the best variant to each person.

cards.q: Conversion Lift: statistical best practices
cards.a: ≥ $10,000 in spend, 10% holdout and > 500 conversions for statistically significant results. Recommended data source: Conversions API.

cards.q: What is creative fatigue and how is it fought?
cards.a: Performance drop from overexposure of the same ad. It's fought by diversifying in messaging, visuals and formats.

cards.q: Mobile-first creative: best practices
cards.a: 9:16 aspect ratio, capture attention quickly, audio with captions, respect the safe zone in Reels/Stories. Use Advantage+ creative to adapt per person and placement; diversify to reduce creative fatigue.
